era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is spreading across Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is consolidating control over the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans; the Spanish conquest of the Americas is accelerating; the Ming Dynasty is stable; and the Reformation-Catholic divide is deepening into institutional conflict.

events.title: Sack of Rome by Imperial Forces
events.description: On 6 May 1527, imperial forces under the command of the Constable of Bourbon sacked Rome, the capital of the Papal States and the center of Catholic Christianity. The sack was ostensibly in retaliation for Pope Clement VII's alliance with France against Charles V, but it was also a consequence of the broader conflict between the Holy Roman Emperor and the Papacy. The sack resulted in widespread looting, destruction, and religious desecration. Pope Clement VII was forced to flee and was effectively imprisoned in Castel Sant'Angelo. The sack shocked Catholic Europe and demonstrated the vulnerability of the Papacy to secular powers. It was one of the most traumatic events in Renaissance Rome and had profound consequences for the Papacy's political authority.
key_figures: Constable of Bourbon (commander of imperial forces, killed in sack)
Francis I (King of France, rival to Charles V)

sources.name: Guicciardini, Francesco. History of Italy (1540, based on eyewitness accounts)

events.certainty_note: The sack's date, circumstances, and consequences are well-documented in contemporary sources and near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Part of Holy Roman Empire-Papacy conflict (1527–1529)

events.title: Battle of Khanua (Mughal Empire vs. Rajput Confederacy)
events.description: The Battle of Khanua (16 March 1527) was fought between Babur's Mughal forces and a confederacy of Rajput kingdoms led by Rana Sanga. Babur's forces defeated the Rajput confederacy, consolidating Mughal control over northern India. The victory was significant because it demonstrated the superiority of Mughal military tactics and artillery over traditional Indian warfare. The battle secured Babur's position as the founder of a stable empire in India and prevented the Rajputs from uniting against Mughal rule. The victory was celebrated in Mughal chronicles and became a defining moment in the early Mughal Empire.

sources.name: Babur, Zahir ud-Din Muhammad. Baburnama (autobiography, primary)
Abul Fazl. Akbarnama (compiled 1590s, based on earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: The battle's date and outcome are confirmed by the Baburnama and Mughal court records.
cross_references: Part of Mughal consolidation of power (1526–1530)

events.title: Henry VIII's Break with Rome (Theological Dispute)
events.description: In 1527, King Henry VIII of England formally requested a papal annulment of his marriage to Catherine of Aragon from Pope Clement VII. The Pope, who was effectively a prisoner of Charles V (Catherine's nephew) following the Sack of Rome, refused to grant the annulment. This refusal set in motion the events that would lead to the English Reformation. Henry's desire for an annulment was driven by his need for a male heir and his attraction to Anne Boleyn. The Pope's refusal led Henry to break with Rome and establish the Church of England with himself as Supreme Head. The year 1527 marks the beginning of the English Reformation.
key_figures: Anne Boleyn (Henry's second wife, not yet married)

sources.name: Letters and State Papers of Henry VIII

events.certainty_note: Henry's request for annulment and the Pope's refusal are well-documented in contemporary letters and diplomatic correspondence.
cross_references: Led to 1531-003 (Henry VIII recognized as Supreme Head of Church)

events.title: Ottoman Consolidation in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: In 1527, the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent continued to consolidate its control over the Eastern Mediterranean. Ottoman naval forces, under the command of Barbarossa (Khair ad-Din), were establishing Ottoman dominance in the Mediterranean, challenging Venetian and Spanish naval power. The Ottomans captured several Venetian-held islands and fortifications, expanding their territorial reach. This period marked the height of Ottoman naval power and the beginning of Ottoman-Spanish rivalry in the Mediterranean. The year 1527 was part of a broader period of Ottoman expansion that would continue throughout Süleyman's reign.
key_figures: Charles V (Holy Roman Emperor, Spanish king)

sources.name: Ottoman court records and naval accounts

events.certainty_note: The broader Ottoman expansion is well-documented, though specific events in 1527 are less precisely dated than major sieges or battles.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's Death (Ming Dynasty)
events.description: Note: The Zhengde Emperor died in 1521, not 1527. In 1527, the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567) was consolidating his authority after a period of regency and eunuch influence. The Jiajing era was marked by administrative reforms, military campaigns against Mongol incursions, and the rise of the Grand Secretariat. In 1527, the court was dealing with persistent piracy along the coast (the Wokou raids) and managing tributary relations. The year falls within a period of relative stability after the tumultuous Zhengde reign.
key_figures: Yan Song (Grand Secretary, rising to prominence)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records are well-preserved and provide clear documentation of the Jiajing Emperor's reign.

events.title: Pizarro's Expedition Begins Exploration of Peru
events.description: In 1527, Francisco Pizarro began his expedition to explore and eventually conquer Peru. Pizarro had received a royal charter from the Spanish Crown to conquer the lands south of Panama. The expedition began with a small force of Spanish conquistadors and indigenous allies. Pizarro was seeking the legendary wealth of the Inca Empire, which had been reported by earlier explorers. The expedition would take several years of exploration and reconnaissance before the actual conquest began in 1532. The year 1527 marks the beginning of the Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire.

sources.name: Pizarro, Francisco. Letters to the Spanish Crown
Cieza de León, Pedro. Crónica del Perú (1553)

events.certainty_note: The expedition's general timeline is well-documented, though specific events in 1527 are less precisely dated than major encounters.
cross_references: Part of Spanish conquest of Inca Empire (1532–1572)

disconfirming_evidence: The Sack of Rome is sometimes portrayed as a turning point in the Papacy's political authority, but the Pope's temporal power had already been declining for centuries. The significance of the sack was more symbolic than substantive in terms of long-term papal authority. The Battle of Khanua is sometimes overstated as a decisive victory; while it was important, Mughal consolidation of power in India took several more decades. Henry VIII's break with Rome in 1527 was not immediate; the formal break occurred in 1534, and the process was gradual. Ottoman consolidation in the Eastern Mediterranean in 1527 was part of a longer process that had begun earlier.
historiographic_note: 1527 is well-documented for European history due to abundant primary sources (diplomatic correspondence, chronicles, royal records). The Sack of Rome is particularly well-documented due to its shock value and the numerous eyewitness accounts. Ottoman history is recorded in court records and diplomatic correspondence. Chinese history is documented through the Ming Shilu. The Spanish conquest of the Americas is documented in Spanish primary sources and later compilations, though indigenous perspectives are mediated through Spanish chroniclers. Mughal history is documented through the Baburnama and court records. Western-centric bias is present; Ottoman and Asian sources are less abundant in Western archives.

graph_edges.note: The Sack of Rome in 1527 led to negotiations and aftermath in 1528
Henry VIII's request for annulment in 1527 led to Wolsey's fall in 1529
Babur's victory at Khanua in 1527 consolidated the Mughal Empire, which Humayun would inherit in 1530
Pizarro's expedition beginning in 1527 continued in 1528 and led to the conquest of the Inca Empire
